Most companies think their biggest legal risk sits in their contracts. 

It doesn’t. 

It sits in their recruitment process. 

The CNIL has announced its 2026 enforcement priorities. 

One focus stands out: 
Automated decision-making in recruitment. 
This is not marginal. 

Many organisations now rely on: 
• CV screening tools 
• candidate scoring systems 
• algorithmic filtering 
Often without a clear audit trail. 

The risk is not only GDPR non-compliance. 

It is decision opacity. 

If a rejected candidate challenges the process, 
the question will be simple: 
Can you explain — and justify — the decision? 

For executive teams, this creates a structural exposure: 

• litigation risk 
• reputational risk 
• hiring bias risk 
• regulatory exposure under GDPR and AI Act 

In practice, very few companies can currently: 

• map where automated decisions actually occur 
• document the logic behind them 
• demonstrate meaningful human oversight 

The real question is not: 

“Are we using AI in recruitment?” 

But: 

“Would we be able to defend how it makes decisions?”

Staff augmentation just quietly became 5.2x bigger than managed services.

Here's what that means for 2026 CTOs.

The shift didn't happen overnight, but it did happen fast. Three years ago, most engineering leaders defaulted to "hire an agency" when they needed outside help. Today, they default to "embed a team."

The reason: control.

With a managed services engagement, you hand over a problem. The provider solves it, invoices you, and leaves. You don't own the code. You don't own the process. You don't own the knowledge.

With staff aug, the engineers work inside your systems. Your codebase. Your tools. Your Slack. Your process. When the engagement ends, everything stays inside your org.

Here's what this shift means practically for CTOs in 2026:

1/ Your AI strategy changes
You can't hand AI tooling over to an agency and expect them to get it right for your context. Staff aug gives you engineers who learn your AI stack and become part of how your team uses it. Agencies give you engineers who use their AI stack and leave when the contract ends.

2/ Your roadmap compresses
Staff aug teams don't need 60 days to understand your architecture, they embed and ship. A well-matched team is productive by week 2. An agency is productive by month 2, maybe month 3.

3/ Your talent pool globalizes
The best specialists for your problem probably aren't in your city. With staff aug, that stops mattering. A senior iOS engineer in Warsaw, a DevOps lead in São Paulo, an ML engineer in Bangalore, all working inside your repos, your sprints, your standups.

4/ Your fixed costs shrink
You scale teams up and down with your roadmap, not with your hiring plan. Product launch in Q2 needs +3 engineers for 4 months? Staff aug. Back to steady state in Q3? Release the augmentation. Your cap table stays clean. Your burn stays predictable.

5/ Your retention problem shifts
FTE retention is a permanent battle. Staff aug is time-boxed. You plan for the knowledge transfer from day one because the engagement has a known end date. You never get blindsided by a sudden resignation.

The companies winning this shift aren't treating staff aug as outsourcing. They're treating it as an extension of their team, with clear scope, clear ownership, and clear handoff.

The CTOs who get this in 2026 are going to ship 2-3x what the ones still defaulting to "hire an agency" ship.

What's your current split between FTE, staff aug, and managed services?

You cannot audit what you cannot see. And most organisations may not care beyond the chat screen.

AI in Audit: Separating facts from fiction. Part 2

Two publications I encourage IA practitioners should read on Auditing AI: COSO's "Achieving Effective Internal Control Over Generative AI" (February 2026) and ISO/IEC 42001, the world's first certifiable AI management system standard (December 2023). Together they define the floor for what AI assurance actually requires. 

The rest of this post draws from both.

1. It starts with an inventory.
Before you can audit AI, you need to know where it is. What tools are deployed. What processes they touch. Who owns them. What decisions they influence. 
In many organisations, that inventory does not exist. Shadow AI, tools adopted informally without IT or risk sign-off, is not a fringe problem. 

2. Then it becomes a controls problem.
AI governance is not a technology problem. It is an internal control problem. It must be designed, operated, and evidenced. 

What Internal Audit should actually be looking at:
- Data quality and lineage. AI outputs are only as reliable as the data fed into them. Every downstream process inherits whatever is compromised at the input stage.
- Human oversight mechanisms. Who, if at all, reviews AI outputs before they influence a decision? Is that review documented and evidenced? Many organisations cannot answer either question.
- Change management as models are updated. AI models drift. An output accurate in January may not be in June. Recalibration schedules and monitoring logic need their own controls.
- Third-party and vendor AI risk. Most organisations are not building AI. They are buying it. The risk sits partly outside direct control, constrained by contract terms and vendor cooperation.
- Bias and fairness testing. Critical wherever AI influences decisions affecting real people. Credit. Hiring. Claims. Complaints. Not an optional add-on.
- Explainability of outputs. Can the organisation explain why the AI reached a decision? Regulators increasingly require it. Most organisations cannot answer it.
- Regulatory alignment. EU AI Act. Data localisation. Sector-specific rules. The regulatory landscape is moving faster than most policies can cope.

3. And here is the honest part.
A joint IIA Foundation and AuditBoard report from February 2026, found that fewer than 40% of IA functions believe they are adequately prepared to detect or respond to AI-enabled risks. Broader AI assurance readiness is lower still, with only 28% of IA leaders confident in their ability to audit AI risks effectively.

That is not a readiness gap. That is an assurance gap the Audit Committee should be asking about directly. Particularly when management's AI rollout is outpacing every control function in the building.

Part 3 will cover the other side. Auditing with AI. Practical use cases in the audit lifecycle. 

Stay tuned.

#InternalAudit #AuditCommittee #ArtificialIntelligence #AIGovernance #IIA

"Indian enterprises won't pay."

We hear this constantly. And at first glance, the numbers support the frustration:

→ Average enterprise sales cycle in India: 9 to 14 months for new AI vendor relationships (per Redseer Strategy Consultants's 2025 SaaS study)
→ Unpaid PoC requests: cited by over 70% of Indian B2B founders as a top friction point
→ Payment delays of 60 to 120 days post-contract are standard in many large enterprise segments

These are real numbers. The friction is real.

But here's what the same data shows about the 30% of Indian AI startups that are winning enterprise deals:

They have, on average, one key differentiator in their commercial team, someone who has previously worked inside the type of enterprise they are now selling to.

Not someone who sold to enterprises. Someone who was inside one. Who knows where the bodies are buried in the procurement process. Who has a personal relationship with the kind of CISO or CDO they need to reach.

This is the insider-to-outsider hire. It's the most underused playbook in Indian deeptech sales.

Instead of hiring a SaaS sales leader who has always been on the vendor side, you hire the ex-Head of Digital Transformation at a Tata group company, someone who used to be the buyer, who can now guide your product and your pitch to fit the buyer's actual decision-making reality.

That profile requires patient, specific search. It doesn't show up on a job post.

When we work with AI founders who are stuck in the 'Skip India' loop, the first question we ask is: who on your team has ever sat on the other side of the table from you?

Usually, the answer is nobody.

That's where we start.

#EnterpriseSales #InsiderHire #AIIndia #TalentIntelligence #QuellSearch

🚀 IT Staffing Trends: What’s Shaping Tech Hiring in 2026

Tech hiring in 2026 is no longer about filling seats—it’s about finding the right skills, faster.

The market is shifting from volume hiring to strategic, skill-based hiring, with demand rising for AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and data talent. Companies are becoming more selective and prioritizing roles tied to growth and transformation.

What’s driving the change?

✔ AI is reshaping teams – companies want high-impact talent that can work with automation tools
✔ Skills over degrees – practical expertise matters more than traditional credentials
✔ Flexible workforce models – contract, hybrid, and augmented teams are growing
✔ Faster hiring cycles – long recruitment timelines are losing relevance
✔ Global talent access – businesses are hiring beyond geography

💡 The companies winning in 2026 won’t be the ones hiring the most people—they’ll be the ones hiring the smartest.

If your hiring strategy still looks like 2024, it may already be outdated.

Role Overview The MLOps is responsible for building and maintaining the full machine learning lifecycle across cloud and on-prem environments. This includes integrating data pipelines, labeling flows, training pipelines, testing frameworks, model optimization, 
 
Key Responsibilities End-to-End MLOps Pipeline Development • Build and maintain automated end-to-end ML pipelines covering data ingestion, dataset management, labeling workflows, training, validation, optimization, deployment, and monitoring. • Ensure ML pipelines run in both cloud and on-prem GPU clusters, with strong reproducibility and traceability. Data Labeling Integration • Integrate data pipelines with labeling platforms and automate dataset creation and quality checks. • Work closely with the Label Manager to enforce labeling quality gates and track dataset KPIs. Training, Testing Optimization • Support AI Engineers by automating training workflows, hyperparameter tuning, experiment tracking, and benchmarking. • Integrate optimization steps performance degradation, anomalies, and operational metrics. • Build dashboards and alerts to monitor model performance across simulation and on-vehicle tests. Collaboration Cross-Functional Alignment • Work closely with: o AI Engineers on training and evaluation o Framework Engineers on model optimization o Application Engineers integrating ML outputs into the autonomy stack o Data Engineering on scalable data and storage architecture o ML Architect on integration and implementation of new components • Drive best practices for MLOps, documentation, and standardized ML workflows across the department. • Steer and guide MLOPS engineers. ________________________________________
 
Required Skills Experience • Bachelor’s or Master’s degree in Engineering, Computer Science, Robotics, or related field. • 5+ years(Tech lead) of experience in data labeling, data operations, data quality, or ML dataset management—preferably in autonomous driving. • Strong programming skills in Python, with familiarity in C++ preferred. • Hands-on experience with MLOps frameworks: MLflow, Kubeflow, Airflow, Argo, Azure ML, Databricks or similar. • Strong understanding of Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, and distributed training. • Experience with data pipeline technologies (Kafka, Spark, Delta Lake, Databricks). • Good understanding of multimodal autonomous driving data (camera, LiDAR, radar).

Jamie Tso and Raymond Sun describe a community building a shared second brain. The people Jamie describes are also candidates. And the tools they have built for themselves do not stay at home during interviews.

A candidate today does not ask whether the company has Zoom or Teams. The answer is taken for granted. 

Now replace Zoom with AI. Most candidates do not yet ask "what is your AI stack?" during interviews, though that is starting to change. 

It is already a routine question, and I have seen conversations where AI-proficient lawyers treat the answer as a clear deal breaker.

Some people worth hiring are turning up with tools they have built themselves, whether AI agents, skill libraries, or retrieval systems trained over evenings and weekends.

Take LegalQuants as an example. Alongside a series of open-sourced prototypes across governance, arbitration, and compliance workflows, they have built a proprietary second brain. It combines a structured knowledge base with a library of /skills that perform specific legal tasks on demand, such as drafting support, document review, or procedural management. Some of those tools run deterministically and do not require a call to an LLM at all, which changes the compliance conversation completely.

After a security and compliance review, tools of that kind could run on company infrastructure without routing a single byte of company data outside it.

Which raises a question that, inside most companies, nobody currently owns: who handles the onboarding of those tools?

Security review knows how to vet software, IP counsel knows how to handle pre-existing works, and knowledge management knows how to share assets across teams. None currently owns the employee who shows up on day one with a working agent and a personal skills library, so the tool ends up sitting unused, getting adopted informally, or being quietly absorbed into the company stack with nobody quite clear on what terms.

The IP question is the difficult one. When something is built on personal time and then refined during work hours against real company problems, does the employee leave with it when they go, or does the company acquire a perpetual licence? Could new hires negotiate a carve-out for pre-existing tooling, the way consulting engagements have IP schedules? And even when the contracts are clear, would employees actually want to share tools they consider part of their professional identity, the way a chef does not hand over his secret sauce?

A company that funds a token allowance and runs a proper onboarding track for employee-built tools will soon have a joining argument, while a company that insists on vendor-only tooling may have a retention problem.

The function that handles all this does not yet have a name. It sits somewhere between IT, Legal, and HR. How soon before it needs to be its own thing?

Top Practice Areas for In‑House Lawyer Jobs in Australia
Based on current Australian hiring trends, these are the legal practice areas companies are hiring for most right now:

Corporate / Commercial (Generalist)
 -Employers want commercially minded lawyers who can handle contracts, governance, projects and day‑to‑day business risk.

Construction/Infrastructure/Projects
 -Always a strong demand for experienced lawyers with solid front-end construction/projects experience

Privacy, Data & Technology Law
 -The fastest‑growing area. Privacy Act reform, data governance, cyber risk and AI regulation are driving strong demand across banks, telcos, tech, retail and healthcare.

Regulatory & Compliance
 -Particularly strong in financial services, funds, energy and healthcare. 

Employment & Workplace Relations
 -Consistently needed across Australian employers

If you’re a lawyer planning your next move, aligning your experience with these areas can make a real difference. If you would like to have a confidential discussion about in-house law jobs in Australia contact Najeeb Nawab on 0401 803 700 or najeeb.nawab@eatonsearch.com.au

Working across Ontario’s tech landscape in 2026, one thing is certain: the "post-pandemic" hiring playbook hasn't just been updated, it’s officially retired.

With the 2026 provincial budget doubling down on AI industrial strategies and a $4B Protect Ontario Account, the stakes for technical talent have never been higher. 

🔍 Key Tech Trends I’m Navigating with My Partners:

The "AI Skills Gap" is Real: It’s no longer just about hiring developers; it’s about securing talent that understands AI governance, cybersecurity, and data sovereignty. 

Agility via Contract-to-Perm: In a leaner fiscal environment, many of my clients are using contract to permanent pipelines to stay agile. It’s the ultimate "try before you buy" strategy that protects budgets while ensuring a perfect cultural and technical fit.

Ontario is positioning itself as a global AI and tech powerhouse. To maintain that edge, we can’t recruit for the job descriptions of 2021. We have to recruit for the infrastructure of 2030. 

To my tech leadership and HR network:  Are you hiring for the role, or for the long term capacity of your organization? Let’s talk strategy.

#OntarioTech #TechRecruitment2026 #AIStrategy #TorontoTech #TalentAcquisition #FutureOfWork

Record confidence. Record caution. That gap is the story.
 
 The Controllers Council Financial Performance Index just hit 149%, an all-time high. Nearly 60% of finance leaders expect stronger performance in 2026 than 2025.
 
 But here's the other number nobody's pairing it with: the Spending-Budget Index dropped 22 points to 88%. CFOs are bullish on revenue and bearish on headcount.
 
 That math creates a structural problem.
 
 Open F&A roles surged 150% in one year. 87% of leaders report a talent shortage. 75% of CPAs are near retirement. And yet permanent hiring plans keep getting trimmed. Gartner's latest shows headcount growth collapsing from 6% to 2%.
 
 So who actually does the work?
 
 Contract finance professionals. Roth Staffing's Q2 2026 data shows this is no longer a stopgap. Companies are running blended models where contract talent handles close, reporting surges, system migrations, and compliance cycles as a permanent layer of the operating model.
 
 The old framing was: "We'll bring in temps until we hire." The new reality is: the hire isn't coming. 50% of CFOs expect AI to reduce finance roles long-term, so they won't commit to permanent headcount. But the work is here now, and it requires people who understand GAAP, ERP systems, and regulatory complexity.
 
 Traditional staffing agencies still treat this like a resume matching exercise. Drop candidates, collect margin, move on. That model breaks when the roles require judgment, not just keystrokes.
 
 Professional services interim staffing is built differently. Curated teams. Managed delivery. Accountability for output quality, not just seat-filling. The difference shows up in ramp time, error rates, and whether the Controller sleeps during close week.
 
 The spending paradox won't resolve itself. Revenue expectations stay high. Headcount budgets stay flat. The only math that works is a flexible execution layer designed for finance, not borrowed from generalist staffing.
 
 CFOs: are you still planning to "hire when the time is right," or have you accepted that the flexible layer IS the plan?

Designing a resilient infosec challenge right now feels like playing poker against someone who can read your mind 😅

This is especially true when folks can use AI models that have become so terrifyingly smart that if you give them even the slightest hint, they can instantly reverse-engineer the entire logic chain.

Kudos to everyone who either tried or was just curious enough to take a crack at the Hidden Token '26 challenge. The goal was to reconstruct a GitHub PAT (github_pat) to push a submission branch to the repository. I'm surprised that people solved it so quick. Maybe I'm a bit behind. 🙃 

The first two parts were easy: the image metadata and the commit history. The 3rd part was an invalid hex color. The final part was rooted in the anatomy of a GitHub PAT. Given a token's standard length and the fact that the last 6 symbols are a checksum (which you can calculate yourself), the remaining 11 Base62 characters can effectively be calculated from an 8-digit text string. This (obviously) had to be some RGBA block like 10204624 (you just needed to try them all).

While I can't name the client just yet, this challenge was a starter project with a major AI lab.

We are developing an RLHF dataset+benchmark designed to train models to conduct advanced security analysis and essentially "think like a hacker": 200-500 high-quality records over the next 4 to 6 weeks. Because I'm not a cybersecurity expert, I am going to be subcontracting the bulk of the technical security work to someone who is. What's why such strange hiring.

There is a good chance this initial sprint will evolve into permanent contract work in the future.

So keep an eye out for updates. Like, subscribe, and as always - inspect everything.

Most business owners think Claude is a chatbot.

It's not. It's six different products.

And most teams are using one of them for work that belongs in another.

That's where money leaks.

Here's the plain-English field guide:
1. Claude Chat. Draft emails, brainstorm, research. The Swiss Army knife.
2. Claude Code. An agent that debugs, refactors, and ships features in your codebase.
3. Claude Projects. A persistent workspace that remembers your client, your tone, your docs.
4. Claude Skills. Custom instructions that standardize how your team runs reports, SOPs, or contracts.
5. Claude Cowork. A desktop app that organizes files, extracts data from PDFs, and runs cross-app workflows.
6. Claude Design. A visual workspace for decks, wireframes, and one-pagers from a prompt.

Here's what most business owners are missing.

Using Claude Chat for everything is like hiring a brilliant generalist to write your code, do your taxes, and design your deck.

They'll try. It won't be their best work.

The real leverage shows up when you match the job to the tool.

If you're still treating Claude as one thing, you're leaving hours and dollars on the table every week.

If you're already routing work to the right Claude for the right job, you're ahead of 95% of your competitors.

I help businesses integrate AI into their operations the right way.

Tools, workflows, and the right model for the job.

Want a map instead of guesswork? Drop a comment or send me a DM. Let's talk.

This is what hiring managers actually want someone like me to bring to their team:

1. Speed without sacrificing clearance quality
In cleared environments (Secret-TS/SCI, poly), the biggest bottleneck is qualified, already-cleared talent. Managers want someone who can shorten time-to-fill without pushing unqualified candidates. My 30% reduction in time-to-fill and consistent monthly hires directly speaks to this—what they want is predictability and velocity in a slow market.
2. Access to hard-to-reach talent pipelines
Anyone can post jobs. Very few can consistently engage passive candidates with Secret-TS/SCI + poly in niche domains like DevSecOps or AI/ML. What stands out is my ability to build and maintain pipelines, not just react to reqs. That’s often the #1 differentiator in GovCon recruiting.
3. Credibility with technical and program stakeholders
Hiring managers—especially in DoD/IC programs—don’t want to “teach” a recruiter the difference between Kubernetes and Docker. They want someone who can:
Pre-screen effectively
Speak their language
Push back when needed
My coverage of software, cloud, cybersecurity, and AI/ML roles signals that I reduce their workload, not add to it.
4. Reliability in mission-critical hiring environments
These roles often tie directly to contract delivery and revenue. A missed hire can mean:
Losing a contract
Failing a program milestone
What they want is someone who consistently delivers under pressure across multiple programs—not occasional success.
5. Full-cycle ownership (not just sourcing)
Many recruiters specialize narrowly. Hiring managers prefer someone who can own:
Intake to offer
Candidate experience
Offer negotiation
Stakeholder communication
My end-to-end experience signals less hand-holding and fewer handoffs.
6. Deep understanding of GovCon mechanics
This is a big one. They want someone who understands:
LCAT requirements
Billable vs. non-billable roles
Proposal vs. funded positions
Clearance crossover rules
That knowledge saves time, avoids bad submissions, and improves win rates on contracts.
7. Consistency across multiple reqs and programs
Handling 3–4 hires per month across concurrent programs shows something hiring managers care about deeply: scalability. They want someone who won’t drop performance when workload spikes.
8. Strong candidate experience in a competitive market
Cleared candidates often have multiple offers. Managers want a recruiter who can:
Build trust quickly
Keep candidates engaged through long processes
Close effectively
This is especially critical at Secret-TS/SCI + poly levels.

𝐁𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐲 𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭. 𝐌𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠, 𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐀𝐈on live project data - under contract pressure, with staff turnover, across changing tech stacks - that's where it falls apart.

Here are 10 tasks I used to pay for that I automated:

1. Proposal drafts. What took an ops / biz dev. person 3 hours now takes Claude 8 seconds.

2. Contract templates. What took a paralegal $300/doc now takes Claude 10 seconds.

3. Outreach playbooks. What took a marketing hire a week now takes Drew one session.

4. Pricing calculators. What took a contractor $5K now takes Claude 2 hours.

5. Email sequences. What took a copywriter $200/email now takes Cleo 5 seconds per variant.

6. Onboarding SOPs. What took a PM 2 weeks now takes Claude 20 minutes.

7. Competitive analysis. What took a researcher $800/report now takes Claude 4 minutes.

8. Presentation deck iterations. What took a designer 3 rounds now takes Claude and me one 30-minute session. Then I hand it off to the designer.

9. Data formatting. What took a VA $30/hour now takes Claude 12 seconds per file.

10. Daily briefs. What took an exec assistant 6 hours a week now takes Claude Calendar + Pat zero.

The pattern: every task on this list is still a job title.

Every job on this list is still being hired for.

Someone is posting a "marketing ops coordinator" role today and budgeting $85K for the same output I automated for $20/month in Claude credits.

This isn't an "AI replaces jobs" post. This is a "stop paying for outputs that cost nothing" post.

The jobs worth hiring for now: judgment, relationships, original thinking, ownership.

Hire for those. Automate the rest.

Reply with what you're automating next.

#AI #Automation #StartupLife #Founders

Gartner just dropped a stat that should make every CEO uncomfortable:

Only 1 in 50 AI investments deliver transformational value.

Only 1 in 5 deliver any measurable ROI.

Meanwhile → companies laid off real people in 2025 to "prepare for the AI transition."

Here's what I'm seeing from the recruiting seat:

→ Firms that cut their mid-level engineers are now paying contract rates to get them back 
→ "AI-first" hiring freezes are quietly ending 
→ Senior talent is more expensive in Q2 2026 than it was pre-layoff

Turns out AI is fantastic at the 80%.

It's the last 20% → the judgment, the client relationship, the "this drawing doesn't feel right" instinct → that still costs a salary.

Companies that treated their workforce like a line item are learning an expensive lesson.

Companies that treated it like an investment are the ones poaching from them right now.

The AI didn't fail. The strategy did.

Are you hiring the talent your competitors let go → or still waiting for ROI that isn't coming?

If I were starting a business from scratch today, I'd do one thing completely differently.

I wouldn't hire my first employee.

I'd hire Claude.

Not because humans aren't valuable — they are. But because the first 12 months of a business shouldn't be spent managing headcount. They should be spent finding what actually works.

Here's what my "Claude-first" startup would look like on Day 1:

→ Customer research: I'd paste every Reddit thread, review, and Twitter rant about my niche into Claude and have it surface the 3 problems no one is solving well.

→ Positioning: I'd draft 10 different landing page angles, then stress-test each one against the objections my ideal customer would raise.

→ Sales: Instead of hiring an SDR, I'd use Claude to research every prospect before a call — their company, their hiring patterns, their recent product launches, their likely priorities.

→ Operations: Every recurring task (invoices, onboarding emails, contract reviews) would have a documented Claude workflow before it ever touched a human.

→ Decision-making: Before any meaningful decision, I'd ask Claude to argue both sides. Not to decide for me — to make sure I've actually thought it through.

The founders who will win the next decade aren't the ones with the biggest teams.

They're the ones who figured out how much leverage a single person can have when they stop doing work AI can do better.

You don't need more people. You need more range.

If you're using AI in your business — in any form — comment "Claude" below. I'd love to see how founders are actually putting it to work.

Pattern recognition is easy to talk about and hard to execute when capital is on the line. Frontier Venture Capital has been executing on that edge since 2002, long before early stage became a crowded trade. Headquartered in Santa Monica with reach into Boulder, Los Angeles, Sacramento, and New York, the firm does not chase gravity, it builds around it. David Cremin shows up with scar tissue, not theory. A founder, a former CEO, a musician who toured under Atlantic, Elektra, and EastWest Records, then an investor who saw DivX before it hit public markets. Scott Lenet, co founder with deep roots in the DFJ Frontier lineage, built and exited SmartFrog.com before most people understood digital rewards. Blair Simpson, now Principal, carries that same hybrid wiring into climate and AI, where creativity meets constraint and turns into signal.

Inside the startup ecosystem, Frontier Venture Capital operates like a first responder. Checks land early, typically between $50K and $500K, but the capital is just the entry point. What they are underwriting is motion. Pre seed through Series A, the thesis centers on computer intelligence, where data, automation, and human behavior collide to reshape how business actually functions. Real time feedback loops that compress decision cycles. Efficient market access that removes friction from distribution. Data and personalization that turn insight into leverage. They are not buying narratives, they are tracking usage before revenue, tension before scale.

The portfolio reads like pressure points. DailyPay converts time into liquidity and edges toward IPO gravity. Project Canary quantifies emissions with clarity regulators cannot sidestep. Syndio forces action in pay equity. Boldin rebuilds retirement planning into something usable. PostSig, Inc. turns contracts into live intelligence. Sensydia pushes cardiac diagnostics into non invasive territory. These are not incremental plays. They are early positions in markets that had not priced disruption.

What sharpens Frontier Venture Capital is not just where they invest, but how they operate. Operator DNA with discipline. Founders are backed for traction, not polish, for proof, not performance. Once capital is deployed, the work begins. Syndicates are built. Introductions engineered. Strategy pressure tested in real time. The firm moves like it remembers the other side of the table, because it does.

If you are building where data, capital, and behavior intersect, Frontier Venture Capital is already paying attention. And if you want to step into companies forming their edge, not protecting it, their portfolio is hiring across engineering, product, and go to market. What is Unsupervised Learning?

If Supervised Learning is the student following a teacher's lesson plan, Unsupervised Learning is the explorer heading into the wild without a map. 🔍

As a Data Scientist, this is where things get really interesting. While everyone asks about "labeled" data, Unsupervised Learning is about finding the hidden structure in data with zero human guidance. No labels, no right answers, just raw data and an AI figuring it out on its own.

Here is how the magic happens:
1️⃣ Input unlabeled data (just the raw info).
2️⃣ Explore the structure as the model looks for similarities.
3️⃣ Discover clusters and natural patterns.
4️⃣ Extract insights that no human told it to look for.
You experience this every time Spotify recommends a new artist or Netflix suggests a show based on your "cluster" of interests. It’s also how we detect credit card fraud (finding the "anomaly" that doesn't fit) and how companies handle customer segmentation to group similar buyers together.

 💡 Think of it like this: Imagine dropping a curious child into a library with no instructions. They’ll naturally start grouping books by their cover color, their size, or the pictures inside. That's unsupervised learning in a nutshell.

The best part? It scales effortlessly because you don't need expensive human labeling. The challenge? The AI doesn't always explain why it grouped things a certain way, so it’s up to us to interpret the "logic."

Headline: Why Reshoring Can Work.

There is a lot of noise about the FCC and the administration pulling Call Center jobs back to the US. Most companies see an operational earthquake that will leave P&Ls in ruins, dreading the $22/hr wage. They’ve been trained by decades of academia to manage labor via dashboard at an arms-length distance.

But they are looking at the wrong map.

The Sears Standard vs. The Digital Gap Americans were trained by the Sears & Roebuck model: Satisfaction guaranteed or your money back. If a tool failed—even if it wasn't the tool's fault—Sears replaced it. No questions asked. Customers signed a social contract to pay more for a product because they were buying a Handshake.

Today, we’ve replaced that handshake with automation exhaustion. We have declassified simple Q&A as no longer requiring human interaction. In doing so, we built the Transactional Trust Gap.

The Venn Diagram of Effort To close that gap, you must identify where customer and agent effort intersect. This is a living process. You must review your call hierarchy and intra-call sentiment for friction at least quarterly; otherwise, you are leading a legacy.

AI is rule-bound. When a minor externality happens—like a hardware color going out of stock—AI lacks the permission to step outside the decision tree. It removes the handshake and kills the brand's soul.

The Brand Guardian Strategy Onshoring only works if you change the model. You want Brand Guardians, not basic agents, handling high-value calls and complex interactions.

Tracking Trust: Grant agents the authority to "Surprise and Delight." Measure what matters most in each call type rather than rigid, one-size-fits-all metrics.

Bring Them Back Incrementally: You did not offshore in a single day. Reshore incrementally by hiring, training, and testing your new solution in high-impact pods.

The Strategic Reset: You might build a model with slightly less immediate profit but significantly more brand loyalty and higher LTV. This recalibration stops the operational tax of brand erosion. Your NPS will move because customers become Ambassadors for Life, and they will pay a premium for the value your operation adds.

The Architect’s Verdict: People didn't shop at Sears because it was cheap; they shopped there because they believed in the brand. Reshoring is the tool to rebuild that trust—but only if you stop managing by dashboard and start managing by the Handshake.

#Onshoring #CXArchitecture #BrandSovereignty #Leadership #BPO #LTV #TransactionalTrustGap

We are hiring at Mithril!

Access to high-performance compute (GPUs, TPUs, and the like) is fundamentally broken: 1. prohibitively expensive and 2. heavily under-utilized.

The providers (mostly publicly traded companies) want financial guarantees that the bet will pay off: contracts with them must be large and long. Only a small set of players (i.e., well-funded "startups") can afford those terms.

The demand from these players is incredibly high—Blackwell GPUs are essentially sold out everywhere—but if you look at the utilization numbers, something is way off. Why?

At Mithril, we believe this is due to a fundamental fact: price control just doesn't scale with AI usage patterns. Small and medium players want access to these advanced chips in unpredictable patterns:
"I need to immediately run this experiment for 48 hours and then again 15 days from now" said a genomics researcher somewhere.
"My inference service explodes in demand every end of the month; I need 500 more chips just for 3 days every month" said the CEO of an Accounting AI platform somewhere else.


This is an incredibly rich field to be part of! At Mithril, you’ll be working to enable millions of research institutions and companies worldwide to leverage one of the pinnacles of human ingenuity—without the usual hassle.

From building our advanced Virtual Machine and high-performance network orchestration, to creating durable, resilient APIs and establishing state-of-the-art consumption principles... there’s just too many GOOD open problems to solve!

Take a look at our openings at https://lnkd.in/g9xkhJHr.
All positions are in-person at our offices in San Francisco and Palo Alto, CA.

🚀 We’re hiring an Engineering Design Specialist to help shape the future of AI-driven innovation!

Job title: Engineering Design Specialist
Job Location: 100% Remote
Application Eligibility: We are open to applications from candidates located in USA.
Contract Assignment

Role Overview
We are looking for experienced engineers with deep design expertise to create challenging, simulation-based design problems for training and evaluating advanced AI models. The role focuses on crafting realistic engineering design tasks where a model must make genuine trade-offs, size components, and iterate against physics-based simulators to meet competing specifications.

Key Responsibilities
Design and validate simulation-based engineering problems that require true design reasoning, with clear constraints and optimization goals.
Deliver complete problem sets including reference solutions, near-miss variants, edge cases, and documentation, ensure appropriate difficulty with multiple failure modes, and collaborate with researchers to refine problems based on model performance.

Domain Tracks
You should have deep design expertise in at least one of the following; expertise spanning multiple tracks is a strong plus.
Electrical & Computer Engineering — analog/mixed-signal IC design, power electronics, RF/microwave, digital system design, FPGA/ASIC, embedded systems.
Mechanical Engineering — structural/thermal/fluid system design, mechanism design, HVAC, vibration/dynamics, manufacturing process design.

Required Qualification:
Master’s or PhD in a relevant engineering/applied science field.
Strong Python (or equivalent) scripting skills.

Interested candidates are encouraged to apply or send me a direct message for more details. You may also feel free to reach out via email
📩 abhishek.h@e-solutionsinc.com
